fix_plans.title: Widen hidden to 256 and train 150 epochs
fix_plans.content: Single combined edit in train.py build_parser(): change `--n-hidden` default from 128 to 256 AND `--train-epochs` default from 100 to 150, leaving n_layers=2, dropout_rate=0.0, n_latent=30, adversarial_classifier=True, scale_adversarial_loss=2.0, n_epochs_kl_warmup=20, lr=1e-3, batch_balance_strength=0.5 all unchanged. This is the round-6 config (which produced kBET 0.7615, our best result) with two simultaneous levers pulled: wider hidden layers (256 vs 128) give the 2-layer encoder/decoder more per-layer capacity to fold the two batches together in latent space, and 50 extra epochs (150 vs 100) gives the adversarial classifier head more time under sustained scale_adversarial_loss=2.0 pressure to break the residual per-batch separation (per-batch kBET is still 0.0/0.0 at round 6 — the encoder still perfectly separates batches, just less so globally). Round 8's tail_mean=0.721 is only 0.04 below the 0.80 PASS threshold, so a small capacity + duration push is the minimal-risk nudge. Round 2 already tried n_hidden=256 alone at 10 epochs and failed, and round 5 already tried 100 epochs alone at n_hidden=128 — neither lever was tried in combination with n_layers=2 + adversarial loss 2.0, which is the configuration that actually lifted kBET to 0.76. ELBO anchor stays healthy: deeper+wider scVI typically lowers ELBO, and the 20-epoch KL warmup keeps reconstruction grounded during the longer run. Do NOT touch record()/conclude() lines, _ProbeCallback, prober.py, or user_analysis().

fix_plans.title: Raise adversarial loss to 5 with lr 5e-4
fix_plans.content: Single combined edit in train.py main(): inside the `vae.train(...)` plan_kwargs dict (lines 500-505) change `"scale_adversarial_loss": 2.0` to `"scale_adversarial_loss": 5.0` AND change `"lr": args.lr` to `"lr": 5e-4` (hard-code the literal, do not touch the --lr argparse default). Leave adversarial_classifier=True, n_epochs_kl_warmup=20, n_layers=2, n_hidden=128, n_latent=30, dropout_rate=0.0, train_epochs=100, batch_balance_strength=0.5 all unchanged. Rationale: round 6 hit kBET 0.7615 but per-batch acceptance stayed 0.0/0.0 — the adversarial signal at scale=2.0 is still too weak to actually break per-batch separation; it only manages to mix the global subsample enough to fool kBET's mean acceptance. Tripling the adversarial loss weight (2.0 -> 5.0) puts much stronger gradient pressure on the encoder to make z batch-invariant. The lr cut (1e-3 -> 5e-4) compensates: a higher adversarial weight makes the combined loss landscape rougher, and a smaller lr keeps the encoder from overshooting and collapsing ELBO. This is a different angle from plan 1 (capacity+duration) — it attacks the same 0.04 gap from the optimization-loss-balance direction rather than the model-capacity direction. ELBO anchor may rise slightly vs round 6 (adversarial pressure slows reconstruction) but the lr cut keeps it bounded well under the 20% utility-floor revert threshold. Do NOT touch record()/conclude() lines, _ProbeCallback, prober.py, or user_analysis().

fix_plans.title: Extend KL warmup to 50 and latent dim to 50
fix_plans.content: Single combined edit in train.py: (a) in build_parser() change `--n-latent` default from 30 to 50 AND `--train-epochs` default from 100 to 150; (b) in main() vae.train(...) plan_kwargs dict change `"n_epochs_kl_warmup": 20` to `"n_epochs_kl_warmup": 50`. Leave n_hidden=128, n_layers=2, dropout_rate=0.0, adversarial_classifier=True, scale_adversarial_loss=2.0, lr=1e-3, batch_balance_strength=0.5 all unchanged. Rationale: round 6 plateaued at 0.76 because (i) the 20-epoch KL warmup finishes too early — the encoder is forced to start compressing toward the prior before the adversarial head has finished breaking batch separation, so a longer 50-epoch warmup keeps adversarial pressure dominant for longer; and (ii) n_latent=30 may be too small to host a batch-invariant manifold alongside cell-type structure for the PBMC 2-batch setup — bumping to 50 gives the latent more room to encode batch variation orthogonal to cell-type variation, which the adversarial head can then push to zero. The 150-epoch budget gives the longer warmup room to converge. This is a different angle from plan 1 (which widens hidden layers) and plan 2 (which scales adversarial loss): it targets the KL-warmup schedule and latent dimensionality instead. ELBO anchor stays healthy — a larger latent and longer KL warmup typically lower ELBO, not raise it. Do NOT touch record()/conclude() lines, _ProbeCallback, prober.py, or user_analysis().
